elements.sent-2411-002: The irrigation-suction process is a common procedure to rinse and clean up the surgical field in minimally invasive surgery (MIS).
elements.sent-2411-003: In this process, surgeons first irrigate liquid, typically saline, into the surgical scene for rinsing and diluting the contaminant, and then suction the liquid out of the surgical field.
elements.sent-2411-004: While recent advances have shown promising results in the application of reinforcement learning (RL) for automating surgical subtasks, fewer studies have explored the automation of fluid-related tasks.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this work, we explore the automation of both steps in the irrigation-suction procedure and train two vision-based RL agents to complete irrigation and suction autonomously.
elements.sent-2411-006: To achieve this, a platform is developed for creating simulated surgical robot learning environments and for training agents, and two simulated learning environments are built for irrigation and suction with visually plausible fluid rendering capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-007: With techniques such as domain randomization (DR) and carefully designed reward functions, two agents are trained in the simulator and transferred to the real world.
elements.sent-2411-008: Individual evaluations of both agents show satisfactory real-world results.
elements.sent-2411-009: With an initial amount of around 5 grams of contaminants, the irrigation agent ultimately achieved an average of 2.21 grams remaining after a manual suction.
elements.sent-2411-010: As a comparison, fully manual operation by a human results in 1.90 grams remaining.
elements.sent-2411-011: The suction agent achieved 2.64 and 2.24 grams of liquid remaining across two trial groups with more than 20 and 30 grams of initial liquid in the container.
elements.sent-2411-012: Fully autonomous irrigation-suction trials reduce the contaminant in the container from around 5 grams to an average of 2.42 grams, although yielding a higher total weight remaining (4.40) due to residual liquid not suctioned.
elements.sent-2411-013: Further information about the project is available at \url{https://tbs-ualberta.github.io/CRESSim/ .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Note to Practitioners---The irrigation-suction process is a surgical procedure for rinsing and cleaning surgical fields.
elements.sent-2411-015: This work tackles automating the process to reduce the workload for surgeons.
elements.sent-2411-016: Our approach is based on two customized simulation environments that can simulate the irrigation and suction process realistically.
elements.sent-2411-017: Two autonomous agents are trained using robot learning approaches in the environments for completing irrigation and suction, respectively, and then transferred to the real world.
elements.sent-2411-018: The agents autonomously control the surgical robot by interpreting the images captured from an RGB camera and the robot's current state, and generate joint movements of the robot.
elements.sent-2411-019: This approach has been tested in physical settings, showing promising results in terms of the individual and combined performance of the agents in executing the full irrigation-suction process.
elements.sent-2411-020: Future work is needed to extend the approach to more practical surgical settings, evaluate the performance under diverse conditions, and enhance integration with existing surgical robot platforms.
elements.sent-2411-021: These include tissue cutting \cite{nguyen2019manipulating,nguyen2019new,shahkoo2023deep , tissue manipulation \cite{tagliabue2020soft,scheikl2022sim,shahkoo2023autonomous,ou2023sim , suturing \cite{varier2020collaborative,chiu2021bimanual,bendikas2023learning , and others, with the overall goal of reducing the workload and enhancing surgeons' skills when performing specific subtasks, achieving what is termed augmented dexterity---the enhancement of a surgeon’s capabilities through robotic assistance under supervision \cite{goldberg2024augmented .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Increasing numbers of these studies are utilizing machine learning approaches, particularly robot learning techniques such as reinforcement learning (RL) and imitation learning (IL), due to their generalizability and reduced manual effort when compared with traditional motion planning algorithms, thanks to their data-driven nature.
elements.sent-2411-023: While these studies show promising results, many rely on manually extracted feature vectors from images as input to the policy, such as tooltip positions and key point locations extracted from markers placed on the surgical instrument or tissue.
elements.sent-2411-024: Although technically feasible, this can be less robust in realistic surgical scenarios, such as when the lighting condition changes and when smoke is present due to energy-based operations.
elements.sent-2411-025: Therefore, relying directly on the raw visual observation captured from the endoscopic camera as input to the policy is usually more desirable, due to the elimination of manual feature extraction and the potential of obtaining an end-to-end policy that takes the sensory input (\eg{image and sensor reading ) and directly produces the desired actions.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Training vision-based agents that take image observations has been extensively explored in general robot manipulation and navigation.
elements.sent-2411-027: A number of these studies rely on a simulation-to-reality (sim-to-real, sim2real) approach, where the agent is first trained in a simulated environment that synthesizes the image observation, and then transferred to the real world.
elements.sent-2411-028: Several recent studies in surgical robotics have focused on this aspect as well.
elements.sent-2411-029: In~\cite{bendikas2023learning , an agent is trained to roll a block using the surgical robot in a simulator that synthesizes $64\times64$ RGB image observations with domain randomization (DR), where the environment parameters such as lighting and camera positions are randomized, and then transferred to the real world directly.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Using a similar approach, Haiderbhai et al.~obtained a vision-based policy for rope cutting \cite{haiderbhai2024sim2real .}
elements.sent-2411-031: In~\cite{scheikl2022sim , the authors achieved sim-to-real transfer for autonomous tissue manipulation by utilizing domain adaptation (DA) using a contrastive generative adversarial network (GAN).}
elements.sent-2411-032: However, one major challenge when applying sim-to-real approaches to surgical task automation is the need to simulate realistic surgical scenes and operations.
elements.sent-2411-033: Unlike simulators for everyday and industrial robotic tasks, which typically involve mostly rigid objects governed by basic principles of mechanics, surgical simulations require a higher level of complexity to accurately model soft tissues, fluids, and dynamic interactions such as cutting and cauterization.
elements.sent-2411-034: The simulation of such physical models and behaviors is already a considerable challenge, but rendering visually realistic images in the simulator to support the training of vision-based agents adds an additional layer of complexity.
elements.sent-2411-035: These complexities are particularly evident in the underexplored area of surgical irrigation and suction.
elements.sent-2411-036: During surgery, blood, debris, and other contaminants are frequently present as a result of procedures like cutting, cauterization, and tissue manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-037: To maintain a clear surgical field, surgeons may follow a two-step process: first, they irrigate the area with a sterile solution, typically saline, to rinse or dilute the contaminants; then, suction is used to remove the fluid along with any remaining debris.
elements.sent-2411-038: This process is usually carried out using an irrigation-suction instrument, which allows the surgeon to both introduce and remove fluids.
elements.sent-2411-039: However, this involves a considerable amount of time and effort and leads to intermittent interruptions of the primary surgical procedure.
elements.sent-2411-040: Therefore, automating this process can help reduce the workload of surgeons.
elements.sent-2411-041: Developing simulated learning environments for the task is challenging, as it involves fluid dynamics and the corresponding interactions for irrigating and suctioning fluids.
elements.sent-2411-042: Fluid mixing should also be considered, as the irrigated solution will be mixed with the existing contaminants.
elements.sent-2411-043: Additionally, developing a vision-based policy involves a greater degree of complexity since the rendering of fluids while mixing them is also required to synthesize visually plausible images.
elements.sent-2411-044: Existing studies that attempt to automate this task primarily focus on the suction phase, particularly blood suction \cite{lai2021verticalized,richter2021autonomous,huang2021model,ou2024autonomous,zargarzadeh2024decision .}
elements.sent-2411-045: In \cite{richter2021autonomous , the authors employ optical flow to track blood flow in real-time and generate a suction trajectory using a computational motion planner to efficiently remove blood while moving upstream toward the bleeding point.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Huang et~al.\ implemented a differentiable position-based fluids (PBF) model of the blood and used model predictive control (MPC) to generate a suction trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-047: In our previous work, a simulated blood suction environment is built based on PBF and is used to train an RL agent that takes a binary image mask of the blood region as input, and outputs the 2D motion of the suction tool \cite{ou2024autonomous .}
elements.sent-2411-048: However, manual feature extraction is still needed to obtain a binary mask representing the area of the blood.
elements.sent-2411-049: To the best of the authors' knowledge, none of the existing studies have developed an agent relying on raw RGB images without manual feature extraction, and the automation of irrigation has not been explored in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-050: In this work, we develop vision-based agents that autonomously complete both the two steps during surgical irrigation and suction.
elements.sent-2411-051: To achieve this, we build a novel surgical robot learning platform for the da Vinci Research Kit (dVRK) that integrates robot learning capabilities and visually realistic fluid simulations and rendering.
elements.sent-2411-052: We design irrigation and suction learning environments in the simulator that resemble the real-world setup.
elements.sent-2411-053: Vision-based agents are trained in the simulator with domain-randomized parameters and carefully designed rewards, learning curricula, and human demonstrations, and are then transferred to the real world.
elements.sent-2411-054: The main contributions of this work are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-055: We propose CRESSim-ML, a surgical robot learning platform for the dVRK, built on our previous work (CRESSim) \cite{ou2024realistic .}
elements.sent-2411-056: The platform is developed with Unity, ML-Agents, and PhysX 5 SDK, and incorporates the actual dVRK Master Tool Manipulator (MTM) for collecting human demonstrations through teleoperation.
elements.sent-2411-057: Based on CRESSim-ML, simulated learning environments are created for irrigation and suction using the Patient Side Manipulator (PSM) from the dVRK and the EndoWrist Suction/Irrigator.
elements.sent-2411-058: Screen-space fluid rendering is implemented and adapted to simulate realistic visual effects including fluid mixing.
elements.sent-2411-059: With carefully designed reward functions, DR, curriculum learning (CL), and IL, two vision-based agents for irrigation and suction are trained in the simulator for controlling the robot in joint space to complete the tasks.
elements.sent-2411-060: We conduct real-world experiments to validate the sim-to-real transfer of the trained agents and analyze their performance quantitatively.
elements.sent-2411-061: To the best of the authors' knowledge, this is the first study to investigate the automation of the two-step irrigation and suction processes.
elements.sent-2411-062: It is also one of the few studies that use a learning-based approach to automate the motion of the surgical robot in joint space based on RGB image observations without additional feature extraction.
elements.sent-2411-063: The overall framework is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:framework .}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Surgical Subtask Automation and Augmented Dexterity
elements.sent-2411-064: A large number of recent studies aim to achieve subtask automation in surgeries using surgical robotic systems, with a long-term goal of increasing the level of autonomy in surgeries, similar to autonomous vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-065: The concept where surgical subtasks are controlled by a robot under surgeons' supervision is recently referred to as augmented dexterity \cite{goldberg2024augmented .}
elements.sent-2411-066: Examples of these studies include tissue cutting \cite{nguyen2019manipulating,nguyen2019new,shahkoo2023deep , tissue manipulation \cite{scheikl2022sim,shahkoo2023autonomous,ou2023sim , suturing \cite{varier2020collaborative,chiu2021bimanual,bendikas2023learning , blood suction \cite{lai2021verticalized,richter2021autonomous,huang2021model,ou2024autonomous,zargarzadeh2024decision , and vessel manipulation \cite{dharmarajan2023automating .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Numerous studies have also focused on the navigation and control of endoscopes \cite{li20223d,li2022learning,gao2022savanet .}
elements.sent-2411-068: Many of these studies leverage RL approaches \cite{nguyen2019manipulating,nguyen2019new,shahkoo2023deep,scheikl2022sim,shahkoo2023autonomous,ou2023sim,chiu2021bimanual,bendikas2023learning,varier2020collaborative,ou2024autonomous .}
elements.sent-2411-069: However, applying RL to irrigation and suction in surgery has been less investigated, largely due to the complexity of simulating a training environment that involves fluid manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-070: Simulating surgical scenes is challenging compared with daily tasks, due to the multiple types of objects and manipulations involved, such as soft bodies (\eg{soft tissue ), fluids (\eg{blood and other body fluids ), burning, and cutting.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Nevertheless, its applications have been important in two major domains.
elements.sent-2411-072: The traditional usage is for training surgeons, where trainees operate in a simulated surgical environment to improve their skills.
elements.sent-2411-073: Examples of such systems include da Vinci SimNow\footnote{\url{www.intuitive.com/en-us/products-and-services/da-vinci/learning/simnow , VirtaMed LaproS\footnote{\url{www.virtamed.com/en/products-and-solutions/simulators/laparos , and iMSTK\footnote{\url{www.imstk.org .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: A more emerging application of surgical simulation is for training autonomous agents using robot learning methods, where agents are trained in the simulator and transferred to the real world.
elements.sent-2411-076: While it is ideal to use commercial simulators such as the da Vinci SimNow for building training environments for robot learning, it is challenging in practice due to the proprietary nature of these software systems.
elements.sent-2411-077: Many recent studies have proposed open-source simulation environments for surgical robot learning, including dVRL \cite{richter2019open , AMBF-RL \cite{varier2022ambf , UnityFlexML \cite{tagliabue2020soft , SurRoL \cite{xu2021surrol,long2023human , LapGym \cite{scheikl2023lapgym , and Surgical Gym \cite{schmidgall2023surgical .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: However, they are generally inferior to the commercial ones in terms of the types of objects that they can simulate.
elements.sent-2411-079: For instance, AMBF-RL is mainly used for simulating rigid bodies and some soft bodies, without the capability for simulating fluids.
elements.sent-2411-080: Nevertheless, achieving sim-to-real transfer for irrigation-suction requires the simulation of visually plausible fluids.
elements.sent-2411-081: This work is also related to recent advancements in robotic manipulation involving fluids, as well as the development of fluid simulation environments for robot learning \cite{ma2018fluid,babaians2022pournet,xian2022fluidlab .}
elements.sent-2411-082: In \cite{ma2018fluid , the Navier–Stokes equation is used for simulating 2D fluid flow, and an RL model is trained to learn to manipulate a rigid object using a water jet.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Babaians et al.\ used PBF for simulating fluids \cite{babaians2022pournet , which is used to train a robot to pour a glass of liquid.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Authors of \cite{xian2022fluidlab considered a number of fluid manipulation tasks, such as fluid mixing and drawing latte art.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Most of these studies focus on daily tasks such as pouring and beverage mixing, which is substantially different from surgical irrigation or suction in terms of task dynamics.
elements.section-2411-003: Simulated Learning Environments for Irrigation and Suction
elements.sent-2411-086: To simulate irrigation and suction, fluid behavior must be simulated and rendered with visual plausibility.
elements.sent-2411-087: PhysX 5 SDK provides the feature of simulating fluid particles on GPU using position-based dynamics (PBD), and more specifically, position-based fluids (PBF) \cite{macklin2013position .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \subfloat[]{ \includegraphics[width=0.3\columnwidth]{figures/fluid_render/color_diffuse_1.png} }\hfil \subfloat[]{ \includegraphics[width=0.3\columnwidth]{figures/fluid_render/color_diffuse_2.png} } \caption{Particle color diffusion. (a) Initial colors; (b) Colors after 10 steps.} \label{fig:color_diffusion}
elements.sent-2411-088: In PBF, fluid is modeled by small particles with positions $\mathbf{x _i$ and velocities $\mathbf{v _i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: During each simulation step, the velocities of the particles are first predicted based on the external forces $\mathbf{f _{ext (\mathbf{x _i)$, such as gravity: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:pbf_velocity} \mathbf{v}_i \gets \mathbf{v}_i + \Delta\tau \mathbf{f}_{ext}(\mathbf{x}_i),\end{equation} where $\Delta \tau$ is the simulation time step.}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: The next-step positions can thus be predicted by $\mathbf{p _i = \mathbf{x _i + \Delta\tau \mathbf{v _{i}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Finally, the velocities are updated based on the predicted position change of the particles, and the positions are set to the predicted ones: \begin{equation}v_i \gets (p_i - x_i)/\Delta\tau , \quad x_i \gets p_i.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-092: As we would like to mix two types of fluids with different colors when simulating irrigation, the simulation should include color mixing and changes.
elements.sent-2411-093: It is therefore straightforward to label each particle with a color and diffuse the colors between particles based on their spatial proximity.
elements.sent-2411-094: To achieve this, each particle is assigned to a cell in a 3D grid with uniform discrete cells based on its position, also known as spatial hashing.
elements.sent-2411-095: For each particle, all particles in a neighboring region are evaluated and the weighted average of the particle colors is computed to update the particle’s color.
elements.sent-2411-096: Therefore, for each particle $i$ with color $\mathbf{c _i$, \begin{equation}\mathbf{c}_i = \frac{\sum_j\mathbf{w}_{ij} * \mathbf{c}_j}{\sum_j\mathbf{w}_{ij}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Weighting can be done based on the distance between two particles, with $\mathbf{w _{ij = \exp(- \left\lVert \mathbf{p _i - \mathbf{p _j \right\rVert / 2 \sigma^2 )$, similar to applying a Gaussian filter.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Particle velocity can be considered as an additional factor, assuming that particles with higher speed should contribute more during color diffusion: \begin{equation}w_{ij} = \exp(- \left\lVert p_i - p_j \right\rVert / 2 \sigma^2 ) \cdot c \cdot \left\lVert v_j \right\rVert, \; (i \neq j),\end{equation} where $c$ is a constant.
elements.sent-2411-099: In this way, particles that are closer to one another and have a higher velocity can influence each other's color more significantly.
elements.sent-2411-100: This process is repeated at each step using a compute shader on the GPU.
elements.sent-2411-101: An example is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:color_diffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-102: To render the particles as fluid, one common approach is screen-space fluid rendering \cite{cords2009interactive,van2009screen , where the fluid surface is reconstructed from the particles in the view space with depth values and surface normals, and then rendered directly in the screen space with per-fragment lighting.}
elements.sent-2411-103: However, in its most simplistic form, this approach does not consider multiphase fluid particles, such as with different materials and colors.
elements.figure-2411-003: \subfloat[\label{fig:fluid_demo_1}]{ \includegraphics[width=0.24\textwidth]{figures/fluid_demo/fluid_rendering_demo_2.png} }\hfil \subfloat[\label{fig:fluid_demo_2}]{ \includegraphics[width=0.24\textwidth]{figures/fluid_demo/fluid_rendering_demo_3.png} }\hfil \subfloat[\label{fig:fluid_demo_3}]{ \includegraphics[width=0.24\textwidth]{figures/fluid_demo/fluid_rendering_demo_4.png} }\hfil \subfloat[\label{fig:fluid_demo_4}]{ \includegraphics[width=0.24\textwidth]{figures/fluid_demo/fluid_rendering_demo_7.png} } \caption{Example fluid simulation and rendering scene. Fluids generated from two fluid sources of different colors are mixed together.} \label{fig:fluid_demo}
elements.sent-2411-104: However, rendering should account for the different colors of each particle in our case.
elements.sent-2411-105: To achieve this, an additional color extraction pass is added to the regular screen-space rendering scheme after the surface depth texture is extracted, which simply extracts the colors of particles that are closest to the fluid surface.
elements.sent-2411-106: This process is similar to \cite{zhang2024real , where the authors propose using a separate pass to extract the colors based on the thickness of the fluid.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The difference is that we do not consider any particle colors apart from the surface ones.
elements.sent-2411-108: This eliminates the need for a thickness pass to extract the fluid thickness and is a valid simplification without losing much visual plausibility thanks to the previously discussed color diffusion scheme.
elements.sent-2411-109: We use anisotropy ellipsoids discussed in \cite{xu2023anisotropic when reconstructing the fluid surface and a narrow range filter \cite{truong2018narrow to smooth it.}}
elements.sent-2411-110: An example fluid simulation and rendering scene is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:fluid_demo .}
elements.sent-2411-111: The overall fluid rendering steps are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:render_flow .}

elements.subsection-2411-006: Learning Environments for Irrigation and Suction
elements.sent-2411-122: Based on CRESSim-ML, two simulated learning scenes are built for training agents to complete the irrigation and suction tasks autonomously and to achieve sim-to-real transfer.
elements.sent-2411-123: The overall configurations of both scenes are similar, consisting of a PSM robot with the Suction/Irrigator, a tissue container where fluids are present, a camera for generating image observations, and a background tabletop, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:sim_env_overview a.}
elements.sent-2411-124: To ensure the trained policy can be applied to the real-world setup, the simulated scenes are designed to resemble the real world, as will be discussed in Section~\ref{sec:experiments .}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.7\columnwidth]{figures/simulation/sim_env_overview.pdf} \caption{Simulated training environments. (a) Overview of the scene setup; (b) Irrigation learning environment; (c) Suction learning environment.} \label{fig:sim_env_overview}
elements.sent-2411-125: In this task, we consider the problem of diluting condensed contaminants, such as clotted blood, by irrigating liquid from the Suction/Irrigator.
elements.sent-2411-126: While transparent saline is generally used in practice, we consider irrigating a red-colored liquid as a simplification.
elements.sent-2411-127: This ensures all liquid added into the container is distinguishable from the tissue and can be further suctioned by the suction agent in real-world experiments.
elements.sent-2411-128: The simplification is reasonable since saline usually turns red instantly in practice after being irrigated due to the presence of blood.
elements.sent-2411-129: In each episode, a random tissue shape is generated in the container, as will be elaborated in Section~\ref{sec:randomization_and_cl .}
elements.sent-2411-130: A random amount of fluid block with high cohesion and friction and a dark red color that simulates a clotted blood area is dropped onto the tissue.
elements.sent-2411-131: The goal is for the agent to control the PSM's joint-space motion, move the end-effector (EE) toward the dense blood area, and irrigate liquid with lighter color and less dense fluid properties.
elements.sent-2411-132: When both types of liquid mix with each other, colors diffuse between particles as discussed in Section~\ref{sec:fluid_sim_render .}
elements.sent-2411-133: Additionally, each particle is initially assigned a value indicating whether it belongs to the clotted blood or the irrigation liquid.
elements.sent-2411-134: This value is also diffused across particles, providing information on the number of particles affected by irrigation.
elements.sent-2411-135: A threshold is used when determining whether the dense blood particles have been affected.
elements.sent-2411-136: After an enough number of blood particles are affected, the task is considered completed.
elements.sent-2411-137: A screenshot from the irrigation environment is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:sim_env_overview b.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Observations and actions\; The observation consists of both visual and vector parts.
elements.sent-2411-139: The visual part includes the RGB image from the camera and the initial frame of the camera, which provides information about the initial location of the dense blood.
elements.sent-2411-140: The vector observation includes current robot joint positions and a number indicating whether the robot is in contact with the tissue, which is decided by measuring the EE link's incoming joint force and torque.
elements.sent-2411-141: The vector observation is stacked with values from the previous 3 steps to include historical motion data.
elements.sent-2411-142: The actions are the incremental movements of the robot joints, along with a control signal to toggle the irrigation on and off.
elements.sent-2411-144: Reward function\; The reward function is composed of several parts, including a reward proportional to the number of particles affected by irrigation at each step, a reward when the EE moves horizontally closer to the blood, and a completion reward.
elements.sent-2411-145: A reward is also given for activating irrigation when the EE is close to the blood, and deactivating it when they are not close.
elements.sent-2411-146: Conversely, it penalizes activating irrigation when distant or deactivating it when near.
elements.sent-2411-147: This encourages the agent to only turn on the irrigation while the EE is close to the blood.
elements.sent-2411-148: Without these, the agent can easily learn a suboptimal solution of always turning on irrigating, which is not a desired behavior in practice.
elements.sent-2411-149: Additionally, penalties are given when the EE's orientation deviates from being vertical, and when the EE is in contact with the tissue, to encourage a safer behavior of the agent in the real world.
elements.sent-2411-150: For clarity, the reward function can be expressed by the summation of the reward features $\psi^{r _i$ multiplied by their weights $w^{r _i$: \begin{equation}r = \sum_i \psi^{r}_i w^{r}_i,\end{equation} as listed in Table~\ref{tab:reward_fun .}}}

elements.sent-2411-188: Curriculum learning (CL) is a common technique to break down a complex task into simpler, more manageable stages and is a well-established method in RL \cite{narvekar2020curriculum .}
elements.sent-2411-189: We further utilize CL when training the irrigation agent by designing a two-lesson curriculum.
elements.sent-2411-190: In the first lesson, the irrigation reward is turned off and the agent only learns to approach the blood area without turning on the irrigation, and then turn it on when the EE is close enough to the blood.
elements.sent-2411-191: Additionally, we do not end the episode when enough particles are affected by irrigation, and no task completion reward is provided.
elements.sent-2411-192: In the second lesson, we restore the original task rewards to let the agent learn the actual task.
elements.sent-2411-193: Between these two lessons, we include transitional lessons where both task configurations (with and without the irrigation reward) are randomly sampled according to a proportion.
elements.sent-2411-194: Overall, the proportion of the actual task increases, gradually guiding the agent toward mastering the complete task through CL.
elements.sent-2411-195: Its effectiveness will be discussed in Section~\ref{sec:training_results .}
elements.sent-2411-196: We further investigate the effectiveness of leveraging expert demonstrations during training.
elements.sent-2411-197: Two IL methods are used, including behavior cloning (BC) and generative adversarial imitation learning (GAIL).
elements.sent-2411-198: BC is applied by adding an additional policy loss during training to encourage generating actions close to the demonstrations: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{BC} = \mathbb{E}_{(\mathbf{s}, \mathbf{a}) \sim \mathcal{D}_{E}} \left[ \ell\left( \pi(\mathbf{s}), \, \mathbf{a} \right) \right],\end{equation} where $\mathcal{D _{E}$ is the expert demonstration dataset, $\mathbf{s}$ and $\mathbf{a}$ are the state and action, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-199: A mean squared error (MSE) loss is used for continuous actions and a cross-entropy loss is used for discrete actions.
elements.sent-2411-200: GAIL is used together with RL to provide additional reward signals that encourage the agent to behave similarly to the demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-201: A discriminator network $D$ is trained to distinguish expert and agent trajectories during training: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{GAIL} = \: \mathbb{E}_{(\mathbf{s},\mathbf{a})\sim \mathcal{D}_E} \left[ \log D(\mathbf{s},\mathbf{a}) \right] + \mathbb{E}_{(\mathbf{s},\mathbf{a})\sim \mathcal{D}_A} \left[ \log (1 - D(\mathbf{s},\mathbf{a})) \right], \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{D _A$ is the agent-generated trajectory buffer.}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation} L_{GAIL} = \: E_{(s,a)\sim D_E} \left[ \log D(s,a) \right] + E_{(s,a)\sim D_A} \left[ \log (1 - D(s,a)) \right], \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-202: The output of the discriminator is then used as an auxiliary reward in addition to the actual environment reward $r^{env}$ with a weighting factor $w$: \begin{equation}r = r^{env} - w \log(1 - D(\mathbf{s},\mathbf{a})).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-203: A variation of GAIL is used in this study, where only the current observation is used by the discriminator, as it tends to result in more stable training.
elements.sent-2411-204: Both BC and GAIL are commonly used with RL to take advantage of expert demonstrations and are implemented as part of ML-Agents.
elements.sent-2411-205: During the initial stage of training, regular RL optimization steps are followed by additional BC steps using $\mathcal{L _{BC}$ with a separate optimizer.}
elements.sent-2411-206: The learning rate decays linearly to zero, after which no BC steps will be carried out.
elements.sent-2411-207: The auxiliary reward from GAIL is added to the environment reward throughout training.
elements.sent-2411-208: To collect demonstrations with different characteristics and to reduce human effort, two types of demonstrations are collected through both scripted policies and human teleoperation in the simulated environment.

elements.sent-2411-318: There are several considerations of computational performance.
elements.sent-2411-319: To allow efficient simulation, a soft body was not used to simulate the tissue although PhysX 5 allows soft body simulation using the finite element method (FEM).
elements.sent-2411-320: FEM is computationally intensive and requires more GPU resources and processing time.
elements.sent-2411-321: We could not create a large number of parallel training environments when using FEM soft bodies, nor were we able to achieve efficient training in terms of wall-clock time.
elements.sent-2411-322: Instead, a rigid body is used to simulate the tissue as a trade-off, although it may enlarge the sim-to-real gap.
elements.sent-2411-323: We have also considered the observation of historical images to capture the temporal information.
elements.sent-2411-324: However, considering the large rollout buffer size and the usage of demonstration data, a much larger amount of memory would be needed.
elements.sent-2411-325: In the current setting, the memory usage is already more than 30 gigabytes with IL for irrigation, making it impractical to stack the image observations with historical ones.
elements.sent-2411-326: To solve this issue, either a much larger memory should be used, or ML-Agents must be modified to allow streaming of the rollout data.
elements.sent-2411-327: In this work, no depth information is provided to the agent.
elements.sent-2411-328: Ideally, it may be possible to achieve a better performance when depth is taken into account.

elements.sent-2411-337: In this work, we examined the problem of autonomous irrigation and suction in MIS using RL.
elements.sent-2411-338: To allow sim-to-real transfer, a new surgical robot learning simulation framework was built using Unity and PhysX 5, which enables the development of the simulated learning environments for irrigation and suction.
elements.sent-2411-339: With DR and carefully designed reward functions, combined with CL and IL, two vision-based agents were trained to autonomously complete irrigation and suction.
elements.sent-2411-340: We found that the irrigation agent achieves a much better performance when a curriculum is used, and that IL helps improve the suction agent but not the irrigation one.

elements.sent-2411-380: Among them, a number of recent studies consider GPU-accelerated simulation, which allows large-scale parallel training and significantly boosts training efficiency and performance.

elements.sent-2411-384: There are a number of real-time fluid simulation and animation techniques, such as smoothed-particle hydrodynamics (SPH), material point method (MPM), fluid implicit particle (FLIP), and PBF \cite{wang2024physics .}
elements.sent-2411-385: Among these methods, PBF is one of the most computationally efficient and numerically stable ones due to its consideration of positional constraints instead of relying on force integration.
elements.sent-2411-386: This makes it particularly well-suited for large-scale trial-and-error learning, enabling massive parallel training, such as in \cite{babaians2022pournet , while avoiding pauses or time rewinds caused by numerical instability.}
elements.sent-2411-387: For fluid rendering, there are two main technical approaches: (a) mesh-based surface reconstruction, and (b) screen-space rendering.
elements.sent-2411-388: The first approach reconstructs a surface mesh from fluid particles or level sets using techniques such as marching cubes \cite{muller2009fast .}
elements.sent-2411-389: However, this approach is generally more computationally expensive than the screen-space method, which approximates the fluid surface directly in image space using depth maps or normal reconstruction techniques \cite{cords2009interactive,van2009screen,xu2023anisotropic .}
elements.sent-2411-390: This is widely used in real-time applications, often in combination with PBF to achieve visually plausible results with less computation.
elements.sent-2411-391: Although PhysX 5 includes an existing PBF implementation, it focuses solely on numerical computation without rendering considerations.

elements.sent-2411-408: In this work, we treat irrigation and suction as two sub-tasks completed by two separate agents, instead of training a unified agent that completes the full process.
elements.sent-2411-409: A key challenge in training a unified agent is defining an effective reward function.
elements.sent-2411-410: While the purpose of suction is well-understood, the goal of irrigation is usually ambiguous when described mathematically.
elements.sent-2411-411: If the reward is based solely on complete contaminant removal, the agent will learn to prioritize suction alone, even though irrigation is beneficial for making the contaminant less sticky.
elements.sent-2411-412: In fact, without irrigation, it is still possible to suction and remove most contaminants in practice, as long as the Suction/Irrigator is in close contact with the contaminant.
elements.sent-2411-413: However, irrigation not only aids in dilution but also prevents contaminants from sticking within the Suction/Irrigator.
elements.sent-2411-414: Therefore, considering two separate sub-tasks is a more natural choice.
elements.sent-2411-415: Moreover, separating the agents provides practical flexibility.
elements.sent-2411-416: For instance, in applications like blood suction (as explored in previous studies, \eg{\cite{richter2021autonomous,huang2021model,ou2024autonomous ), our suction agent could be deployed independently without the irrigation phase.}}
elements.sent-2411-417: It is also worth noting that in general, we consider models that observe a vision input from the camera and a vector input of the robot joint, and output the joint-space position increment.
elements.sent-2411-418: Since the action is in the joint space, the observation of the current joint positions is needed.
elements.sent-2411-419: This allows direct mapping to low-level controller commands in the joint space without requiring an inverse kinematic solver.
elements.sent-2411-420: In this work, using Cartesian end-effector (EE) position increments as agent actions is also impractical, as the PSM combined with the Suction/Irrigator has only 5 DoFs.
elements.sent-2411-421: A Cartesian space action requires 6-DoF position increments, but since the robot is kinematically constrained, it lacks the necessary DoFs to support this approach.
elements.sent-2411-422: For example, if the agent's output specifies pure translation of the end-effector along the X-axis while keeping all other DoFs at zero, the system will typically introduce an unintended rotational component.
elements.sent-2411-423: This occurs because the robot has only 5 DoFs, making it kinematically impossible to achieve arbitrary 6-DoF Cartesian motion.
elements.sent-2411-424: As a result, the robot moves in a way that satisfies its own constraints, violating the agent's intended zero-motion in other DoFs.
elements.sent-2411-425: Ideally, there should be no significant performance difference between joint-space and Cartesian actions, provided the current robot position is included in the observation.
elements.sent-2411-426: Furthermore, we employ incremental actions rather than absolute joint positions, as is common in RL-based control work, such as in \cite{tagliabue2020soft,xu2021surrol,shahkoo2023deep,xian2022fluidlab .}
elements.sent-2411-427: Incremental actions mitigate training challenges caused by absolute-value scaling, where small workspace variations become difficult to learn.
elements.sent-2411-428: Additionally, they help maintain consistent execution timing, preventing large, stepwise motions that could disrupt inference-time control consistency.

elements.sent-2411-498: Further analysis of task failure cases and a comparison between agents' performance and human operation may be necessary before considering surgical applications, which are beyond the scope of this research.
elements.sent-2411-499: Further extension of this work to actual surgeries will also need additional development of realistic surgical scenes and re-training of the agents, as this work considers a relatively simple environment setup without actual surgical components, such as phantom tissue.
elements.sent-2411-500: With the simulation capability introduced in this work, transitioning to more realistic surgical scenes--including those beyond minimally invasive surgery--is straightforward, particularly when simulating body fluids.
elements.sent-2411-501: To allow sim-to-real transfer, a new surgical robot learning simulation framework was built using Unity and PhysX 5, which enables the development of simulated learning environments for irrigation and suction.
